Chapter 7 — Structure & Synthesis of Alkenes

Comprehensive mastery guide · built from the original lecture deck (Dr. Nisreen Alhaj)

Sections 7-1 through 7-18 · rearrangements excluded as requested

Full explanations Original figures & mechanisms 40-question interactive quiz Flashcards

How to use this guide

This single file rebuilds your entire CH7 deck into a mastery resource: every topic has expanded explanations, the original figures and mechanisms pulled straight from the slides, exam tips, mnemonics, worked calculations, then flashcards and a 40-question interactive quiz that grades itself.

  • Use the left menu to jump around (it tracks where you are).
  • Click any flashcard to flip it.
  • In the quiz, pick answers then hit Check answers — you’ll get a score and a why-explanation on every question.
As you asked, carbocation rearrangements are intentionally excluded. Everything else from sections 7-1 to 7-18 is covered.

7-1  Structure of Alkenes

An alkene is a hydrocarbon that contains at least one carbon–carbon double bond (C=C). Because it holds fewer hydrogens than the corresponding alkane, an alkene is called unsaturated. The C=C is the functional group — the reactive heart of the molecule — so almost everything in this chapter flows from understanding it.

The double bond = one σ bond + one π bond

  • Each doubly-bonded carbon is sp² hybridized.
  • The three sp² orbitals lie in a plane 120° apart → trigonal-planar geometry.
  • The leftover, un-hybridized p orbital on each carbon overlaps side-to-side to form the π bond. This sideways overlap is what locks the geometry (no free rotation).
A π bond cannot rotate without breaking. That rigidity is the entire reason cis–trans (geometric) isomerism exists (Section 7-5).

Why the double bond is shorter

sp² orbitals carry more s-character than sp³ orbitals, so the bonding electrons sit closer to the nuclei. Add the extra π overlap pulling the carbons together and the bond contracts:

BondHybridizationLengthNotes
C–C (alkane)sp³1.54 Åsingle, freely rotating
C=C (alkene)sp²1.33 Åshorter, stronger, rigid
If a question asks “which is shorter/stronger,” remember: more s-character + π overlap → shorter & stronger bond, but the π component is weaker than a σ, which is why alkenes are reactive toward addition.

7-2  Elements of Unsaturation (Degree of Unsaturation)

Four names, one idea: Elements of Unsaturation = Degree of Unsaturation = Unsaturation Number = Index of Hydrogen Deficiency (IHD). Each “element” tells you the molecule is missing two hydrogens relative to the fully saturated formula.

  • Saturated = holds the maximum number of H (reacts with no more H₂).
  • 1 double bond = 1 element  •  1 ring = 1 element  •  1 triple bond = 2 elements.

The formula

Elements of Unsaturation = [ (2C + 2) − H ] / 2

where the saturated reference for hydrocarbons is CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. In words: find the max possible H, see how many are missing, and halve it.

This count cannot tell you whether the unsaturation comes from a double bond or a ring — it only gives the total. You need the structure to know which.

Dealing with heteroatoms (atoms that aren’t C or H)

AtomRuleWhy
Halogen (F, Cl, Br, I)Count as hydrogenmonovalent — it just replaces an H
Oxygen (O)Ignore itdivalent — inserts into a chain/bond without changing C or H counts
Nitrogen (N)Count as ½ carbontrivalent — adds only 1 H, vs 2 H per added C
“Halogens act like H, Oxygen is invisible, Nitrogen is half a Carbon.”

Worked examples

C₄H₅Br₃: treat the 3 Br as 3 H → effectively C₄H₈. Saturated C₄ would be C₄H₁₀. Missing 2 H → 1 element of unsaturation.
The slide’s nitrogen example: a compound counted as C₄.₅H₁₁ (N treated as ½ C). Max H = 2(4.5)+2 = 11. The compound shown leads to 11 − 7 = 4 missing, 4 / 2 = 2 elements of unsaturation.
Exam shortcut for a quick sanity check: an aromatic ring (benzene) is always 4 (3 C=C + 1 ring); a C≡C or C≡N is 2; each C=O or C=C is 1.

7-3  Nomenclature of Alkenes

IUPAC naming of an alkene follows the alkane rules with a few double-bond-specific twists.

  1. Pick the longest chain that contains the C=C. The double bond must be in the parent chain even if a longer chain exists elsewhere.
  2. Change the alkane suffix -ane → -ene.
  3. Number from the end nearest the double bond so the C=C gets the lowest locant possible.
  4. In a ring, the double bond is assumed to be between C1 and C2 — no number needed for a single ring double bond.

More than one double bond

# of C=CSuffix
2-adiene
3-atriene
4-atetraene

Use a locant for every double bond, and the prefixes di-, tri-, tetra- before -ene. In rings, give the double bonds the lowest set of numbers.

Alkenes as substituents — “alkenyl” groups

When the C=C sits on a branch, the group is named as a substituent (an alkenyl group):

Common nameStructure
Vinyl (ethenyl)CH₂=CH–
Allyl (2-propenyl)CH₂=CH–CH₂–
Methylene=CH₂ (or a bridging –CH₂–)
PhenylC₆H₅– (benzene ring as substituent)
Vinyl vs allyl trips people up: vinyl = the carbon is part of the C=C; allyl = a CH₂ next to a C=C. Allyl ⇒ “allyl has an adjacent CH₂.”

7-5  Cis–Trans & E–Z Nomenclature

Because the π bond blocks rotation, the two ends of a C=C are locked — giving geometric (cis–trans) isomers. A double bond that can show this is called stereogenic.

The cis–trans system

  • cis = the two like / main groups are on the same side of the double bond.
  • trans = the two like / main groups are on opposite sides.
The simple cis/trans labels break down when each carbon carries two different groups (nothing obvious is “the same”). That’s why we need the rigorous E–Z system.

The E–Z system (uses Cahn–Ingold–Prelog priorities)

  1. On each double-bond carbon, rank its two groups by CIP priority (higher atomic number wins; if tied, move to the next atoms outward).
  2. Look at where the two higher-priority groups end up:
LabelGermanMeaningHigh-priority groups are…
Zzusammentogetheron the same side
Eentgegenoppositeon opposite sides
“Z = same Zide.” If the two high-priority groups are on the same side, it’s Z.

7-7  Physical Properties of Alkenes

A. Boiling points & densities

Alkenes behave a lot like the matching alkanes:

  • but-1-ene, cis-but-2-ene, trans-but-2-ene and n-butane all boil near 0 °C.
  • Boiling point rises smoothly with molecular weight (bigger → more London dispersion forces).
  • More branching → more volatile → lower boiling point (branched molecules have less surface contact).
  • Densities ≈ 0.6–0.7 g/cm³ — lighter than water, so they float and are insoluble in water.

B. Polarity

  • Alkyl groups donate a little electron density to the sp² carbons, giving a small bond dipole.
  • In a cis alkene the two dipoles partly add up → a net dipole → slightly polar.
  • In a trans alkene the dipoles point oppositely and cancel → little/no net dipole.
Cis alkenes have the larger dipole moment, so the cis isomer boils higher than the trans. (Trans, however, is usually the more stable isomer — don’t confuse stability with boiling point.)

Overall alkenes are relatively nonpolar and insoluble in water.

7-8  Stability of Alkenes

A. Heats of hydrogenation — how we measure stability

To compare two alkenes we convert each to the same product (the alkane) and measure the heat released. Adding H₂ across the C=C (hydrogenation) is exothermic; the heat given off is the heat of hydrogenation.

The more stable alkene sits at lower energy, so it releases less heat on hydrogenation → a lower heat of hydrogenation means a more stable alkene.
cis vs trans-2-butene: they hydrogenate to the same butane. trans releases 115.5 and cis releases ~120 kJ/mol; the slide gives the gap as ≈ 11.3 kJ/mol (2.7 kcal/mol) in favor of the more-substituted/trans arrangement — i.e. trans-2-butene is the more stable isomer.

B. Substitution effects — why “more substituted = more stable”

The most stable double bonds carry the most alkyl groups. Two reasons:

  1. Electronic (hyperconjugation): alkyl groups are mildly electron-donating and feed electron density into the π system, stabilizing it.
  2. Steric: bulky groups want to be far apart. An alkane separates them by ~109.5°; a double bond opens that to ~120°, relieving strain — and the most substituted alkene spreads the bulk best.
Stability: tetra- > tri- > di- > mono-substituted > ethylene
Between geometric isomers, trans is usually more stable than cis because its alkyl groups are farther apart (less steric strain).

D. Stability of cycloalkenes & ring strain

  • A ring only changes the stability picture if it introduces ring strain — from a small ring or a trans double bond inside a ring.
  • Rings that are 5-membered or larger hold a (cis) double bond comfortably and react like normal chain alkenes.
  • In small cycloalkenes the cis isomer is more stable (trans would be hugely strained).
  • A ring needs ≥ 8 carbons to support a stable trans double bond (trans-cyclooctene is the smallest isolable one).
  • For cyclodecene and larger, the trans double bond is almost as stable as the cis.

E. Bredt’s Rule

Bredt’s Rule: a bridged bicyclic compound cannot have a double bond at a bridgehead carbon (unless the rings are large). The bridgehead can’t reach the planar, 120° sp² geometry a C=C needs, so such an arrangement is too strained / not stable.

F. Conjugated vs isolated double bonds

TypeArrangementInteractionStability
ConjugatedC=C–C=C (separated by exactly one single bond)π systems overlap & interactextra-stable
Isolatedseparated by two or more single bondslittle interactionnormal
“Conjugated = connected.” One single bond between two C=C lets the π clouds talk to each other, and that delocalization is stabilizing.

7-9  Formation of Alkenes by Elimination

The major way to make alkenes is elimination: two atoms/groups leave from adjacent carbons and a new π bond forms.

MethodWhat leavesConditions
E2 dehydrohalogenationH and X (as HX)strong base
E1 dehydrohalogenationH and X (as HX)weak base / ionizing solvent
Dehalogenation of vicinal dibromidestwo X (as X₂)e.g. Zn or I⁻
Dehydration of alcoholsH and OH (as H₂O)acid catalyst, heat
Dehydrohalogenation = elimination of a proton (H⁺) + a halide (X⁻) from an alkyl halide to give an alkene. It can go by either the E1 or E2 pathway.

7-9A  Zaitsev’s Rule (Regiochemistry)

Zaitsev’s Rule: when more than one alkene can form, the more highly substituted (more stable) alkene is the major product.

This follows directly from Section 7-8: more substituted = more stable, so the transition state leading to it is lower in energy. The Zaitsev product is also called the more-substituted product.

Keep this paired with its opposite (Section 7-12): a normal base → Zaitsev; a bulky base → Hofmann (least substituted).

7-10  The E1 Reaction (Unimolecular Elimination)

E1 = Elimination, 1st order. The rate depends only on the substrate: rate = k[substrate].

Mechanism (two steps)

  1. Slow / rate-determining: the leaving group departs, forming a carbocation.
  2. Fast: a base plucks a proton off a carbon adjacent (β) to the C⁺; that carbon rehybridizes to sp² and the electrons flow into the new π bond.
  • Favored by: 3° > 2° substrates, weak bases, good ionizing (polar protic) solvents, heat.
  • Regiochemistry follows Zaitsev (most substituted alkene major).

A. Competition between E1 and SN1

Any time a carbocation forms it can go two ways — substitution (SN1) or elimination (E1) — so E1 almost always competes with SN1 and you get mixtures.

Solvolysis (“solvo” = solvent, “lysis” = cleavage) is the special case where the solvent itself acts as the weak base/nucleophile and reacts with the substrate.

7-11  The E2 Reaction (Bimolecular Elimination)

E2 = Elimination, 2nd order: rate = k[substrate][base]. It needs a strong base.

Mechanism (one concerted step)

Everything happens at once: the base grabs the β-proton, the new π bond forms, and the leaving group leaves — all in a single step with no intermediate.

  • Reactivity / product distribution reflects the greater stability of more-substituted double bonds → normally Zaitsev.
  • On a tertiary substrate, no substitution is seen: the SN2 path is blocked because the 3° carbon is too hindered for backside attack — so a strong base on a 3° halide gives clean E2.

7-12  Bulky Bases & Hofmann Orientation

You can steer an E2 toward the less substituted alkene by choosing the base.

  1. If a substrate is prone to substitution, switch to a bulky base to suppress SN2 and favor elimination.
  2. Bulky bases (e.g. potassium tert-butoxide, KOtBu) drive eliminations that break Zaitsev’s rule and give the Hofmann product.

Why the Hofmann product forms

Steric hindrance stops the big base from reaching the proton that would give the most-substituted (Zaitsev) alkene. Instead it removes a less hindered proton — usually the one that gives the least substituted alkene, the Hofmann product.

Small/unhindered base → Zaitsev (more substituted).   Bulky base → Hofmann (less substituted).
“Big Base → Baby alkene (Hofmann).” A bulky base makes the smaller, less-substituted alkene.

7-13  Synthesis by Dehydration of Alcohols

Heating an alcohol with acid eliminates water (dehydration) to give an alkene.

  • Catalysts: concentrated H₂SO₄ or H₃PO₄.
  • Le Chatelier trick: distill the low-boiling alkene out as it forms to pull the equilibrium forward and boost the yield.
  • Goes by an E1 mechanism (protonate OH → lose water to a carbocation → lose a β-proton).
  • Obeys Zaitsev’s rule — the more substituted alkene is the major product.

Key mechanism (E1 dehydration)

  1. Protonation: acid protonates the –OH, turning a poor leaving group into –OH₂⁺ (water — an excellent leaving group).
  2. Ionization: water leaves → carbocation (rate-determining).
  3. Deprotonation: a base (often the solvent / HSO₄⁻) removes a β-proton → the π bond forms and the catalyst is regenerated.
Because dehydration runs through a free carbocation, carbocation rearrangements are common in the full treatment. (Per your request, the rearrangement details are intentionally left out of this guide.)

7-14  Stereochemistry of the E2 Reaction

E2 has a strict geometry requirement: the β-H and the leaving group must lie in the same plane (coplanar) so their orbitals can line up as the π bond forms.

GeometryDihedral (H–C–C–LG)Notes
Anti-coplanar (anti-periplanar)180°Strongly preferred — staggered, lowest-energy transition state
Syn-coplanar (syn-periplanar)0°Possible but higher energy (eclipsed); only when the ring/rigid system forbids anti
Default assumption on an exam: E2 goes anti-periplanar. This controls which β-H is removed and therefore the stereochemistry of the alkene produced.

7-18  E1 vs E2 — and Substitution vs Elimination

The big-picture decision table tying together everything (and SN1 / SN2 from before):

ReagentOrderSubstitutionElimination
Strong base / nucleophilebimolecularSN2E2
Weak base / nucleophileunimolecularSN1E1
Because SN1 and E1 share a carbocation, the unimolecular reactions give mixtures you can’t control well — which is exactly why unimolecular reactions are rarely used for synthesis.

E1 vs E2 at a glance

FeatureE1E2
Steps2 (carbocation intermediate)1 (concerted)
Rate lawk[substrate]k[substrate][base]
Baseweakstrong
Substrate3° > 2°3° > 2° > 1°
Stereochemnot specificanti-periplanar
RegiochemZaitsevZaitsev (Hofmann with bulky base)
Competes withSN1SN2
Rearrangementspossiblenone

Rapid-Recall Flashcards

Click a card to reveal the answer. Great for the night before.

Hybridization & geometry of a C=C carbon?
sp², trigonal planar, ~120° bond angles.
C=C bond length vs C–C?
1.33 Å (alkene) vs 1.54 Å (alkane) — shorter & stronger.
Formula for elements of unsaturation?
(2C + 2 − H) / 2 (treat halogen as H, ignore O, N = ½ C).
Heteroatom rules for IHD?
Halogen → count as H  |  Oxygen → ignore  |  Nitrogen → ½ carbon.
Z vs E?
Z = high-priority groups on the SAME side (zusammen). E = opposite sides (entgegen).
Which boils higher, cis or trans alkene?
cis — it has the larger dipole moment.
Which is more stable, cis or trans?
Usually trans — alkyl groups are farther apart (less strain).
Heat of hydrogenation vs stability?
Lower heat of hydrogenation = MORE stable alkene.
Most stable degree of substitution?
Tetra- > tri- > di- > mono-substituted > ethylene.
Smallest ring with a stable trans C=C?
8 carbons (trans-cyclooctene).
Bredt's rule?
No double bond at a bridgehead of a (small) bridged bicyclic system.
Conjugated vs isolated double bonds?
Conjugated = one single bond apart (interact, extra stable); isolated = 2+ single bonds apart.
Zaitsev's rule?
The more-substituted (more stable) alkene is the major elimination product.
E1 rate law & steps?
rate = k[substrate]; 2 steps via a carbocation.
E2 rate law & steps?
rate = k[substrate][base]; 1 concerted step; needs a strong base.
Bulky base gives which product?
Hofmann — the LEAST substituted alkene.
Required E2 geometry?
Anti-periplanar (H and leaving group coplanar at 180°).
Dehydration catalysts & mechanism?
Conc. H₂SO₄ / H₃PO₄; E1 mechanism; obeys Zaitsev.
Why no SN2 on a 3° halide?
Too sterically hindered for backside attack — so a strong base gives clean E2.
What is solvolysis?
The solvent itself acts as the weak base/nucleophile (solvo = solvent, lysis = cleavage).
